title: Fitting electric poultry netting
subtitle: Fox-proofing a free-range flock with a single roll of netting.
excerpt: Electric poultry netting is the most practical fox deterrent for a small free-range flock. A 50-metre roll encloses a decent run, sets up in 20 minutes once you know the system, and moves when the grass needs a rest. Getting the energiser matched to the run and the earth spike correctly placed is the difference between a fence that works and one that does not.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for Laying Hens (2018), predator protection guidance. Gallagher and Hotline energiser manufacturer guidance for poultry fencing applications.

glossaryTerms.definition: The pulse generator unit of an electric fence system. Rated in joules of output — poultry netting needs at least 0.3 J of delivered energy at the fence line to deter a fox reliably. Mains units are more powerful; battery units are portable.

content.text: Electric poultry netting is 112 cm-tall plastic mesh with horizontal conductors that carry the fence pulse. The integrated plastic posts step into the ground at intervals; the whole run assembles without tools. The fence works by giving a sharp shock to any animal that touches the horizontal wires, deterrent rather than containment.

output to the total length of netting. A single 50-metre roll in good condition needs at least 0.3 J of output; two rolls or a long run in long grass needs 0.5 to 1 J. Mains energisers are best where power is available; battery energisers on a 12 V leisure battery last two to four weeks per charge and are suitable for field sites.

Cut back any long grass along the fence line before installing. Wet vegetation touching the netting drains the charge to earth and reduces effectiveness, a fence reading below 2,000 volts at the furthest point from the energiser will not deter a fox reliably. On an established site, strim or mow one week before putting up the netting.

Unroll the netting in a rectangle. Step the integral posts into the ground at 3-metre intervals using your foot on the built-in footpeg; the posts need to lean slightly outward to keep the netting taut at the top. Corner posts go in first, a corner with a loose post collapses the whole run. Connect the energiser to the netting at the gate connector and to the earth spike. The earth spike needs to go into moist ground at least 30 cm deep; a second earth spike 1 metre away significantly improves performance in dry summer conditions.

Use a fence tester (a simple probe-and-LED indicator) on the netting at the far corner from the energiser. A reading above 2,000 volts is the working minimum; above 3,000 volts is comfortable. If the reading drops below 2,000 volts, check for vegetation touching the fence and clear it. A reading near zero despite the energiser running usually means a broken conductor wire in the netting, trace it by walking the fence line and looking for a section that hangs differently.
Introducing the flock to the fence

Turn the fence on before releasing the birds into the new run. Young birds that have never encountered electric fencing will approach and touch it, receive a shock, and generally leave it alone from then on. Do not turn the fence off while birds are in the run, hens that have learned the fence is live will not test it; hens that see it switched off quickly learn it is sometimes safe to push through.

attrs.cue: Fox has pushed through the fence
attrs.cause: Fence voltage was too low, earth spike too shallow or dry, or the fox approached through long grass that was shorting the bottom conductor
attrs.fix: Add a second earth spike, clear all vegetation from the bottom two conductors, and check that the energiser is delivering the rated output. A fox that has breached once will try again; run a second roll inside at 30 cm offset if the site has repeat pressure.

attrs.cue: Fence not holding charge after rain
attrs.cause: Wet vegetation shorting conductors to earth across the whole fence line
attrs.fix: Clear vegetation and check that the bottom conductor is at least 15 cm off the ground. If the ground slopes, the netting will sag in low spots, repost the sagging sections.
